content.text:
The real numbers are not just a field—they are an *ordered* field. 
This means we can compare any two real numbers and say which is larger.

content.title: Ordered Field
content.content:
An **ordered field** is a field $F$ together with 
a relation $<$ satisfying:

1. **Trichotomy:** For all $a, b \in F$, exactly one of the following 
holds: $a < b$, $a = b$, or $b < a$.

2. **Transitivity:** If $a < b$ and $b < c$, then $a < c$.

3. **Addition preserves order:** If $a < b$, then $a + c < b + c$ 
for all $c \in F$.

4. **Multiplication by positives preserves order:** If $a < b$ and 
$c > 0$, then $ac < bc$.

content.text: We define the other comparison relations in terms of $<$:

content.title: Positive and Negative
content.content:
An element $a$ of an ordered field is called:

- **positive** if $a > 0$

- **negative** if $a < 0$

- **nonnegative** if $a \geq 0$

- **nonpositive** if $a \leq 0$

content.title: Properties of Order
content.content:
In an ordered field $F$:

1. If $a > 0$ and $b > 0$, then $a + b > 0$ and $ab > 0$.

2. If $a < 0$ and $b < 0$, then $ab > 0$.

3. If $a < 0$ and $b > 0$, then $ab < 0$.

4. $a^2 \geq 0$ for all $a \in F$, with equality if and only if $a = 0$.

5. $1 > 0$.

6. If $a > 0$, then $a^{-1} > 0$.

7. If $0 < a < b$, then $0 < b^{-1} < a^{-1}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
(a) If $a > 0$, then by (O3), $a + b > 0 + b = b > 0$ by transitivity with 
$b > 0$. For products, use (O4) with $c = b > 0$ to get $ab > 0 \cdot b = 0$.

(b) By (O4), if $a < 0$, multiplying by $b < 0$ reverses the inequality. 
Formally: $a < 0$ implies $a \cdot (-b) < 0 \cdot (-b) = 0$ since $-b > 0$. 
Thus $-ab < 0$, so $ab > 0$.

(c) If $a < 0$ and $b > 0$, then $ab < 0 \cdot b = 0$ by (O4).

(d) If $a = 0$, then $a^2 = 0$. If $a > 0$, then $a^2 = a \cdot a > 0$ by 
part (a). If $a < 0$, then $a^2 > 0$ by part (b).

(e) We have $1 = 1^2 \geq 0$ by part (d). Since $1 \neq 0$ (field axiom), 
we have $1 > 0$.

(f) Suppose $a > 0$ but $a^{-1} \leq 0$. If $a^{-1} = 0$, then 
$1 = a \cdot a^{-1} = 0$, contradiction. If $a^{-1} < 0$, then 
$1 = a \cdot a^{-1} < 0$ by part (c), contradicting $1 > 0$.

(g) From part (f), both $a^{-1}$ and $b^{-1}$ are positive. Multiplying 
$a < b$ by the positive quantity $a^{-1}b^{-1}$ gives $b^{-1} < a^{-1}$.

content.title: Absolute Value
content.content:
The **absolute value** of a real number $x$ is 
defined by
\[
|x| = \begin{cases} x & \text{if } x \geq 0 \\ -x & \text{if } x < 0 \end{cases}
\]

content.text: The absolute value measures the *distance* from a number to zero.

content.title: Properties of Absolute Value
content.content:
For all $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$:

1. $|a| \geq 0$, with equality if and only if $a = 0$.

2. $|-a| = |a|$.

3. $|ab| = |a| \cdot |b|$.

4. $|a|^2 = a^2$.

5. $|a| \leq b$ if and only if $-b \leq a \leq b$ (assuming $b \geq 0$).

6. **Triangle Inequality:** $|a + b| \leq |a| + |b|$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We prove only the triangle inequality; the other parts are left as exercises.

*Proof of (f):* By part (e), it suffices to show that 
$-(|a| + |b|) \leq a + b \leq |a| + |b|$.

We have $-|a| \leq a \leq |a|$ and $-|b| \leq b \leq |b|$ by the definition 
of absolute value. Adding these inequalities:
\[
-(|a| + |b|) \leq a + b \leq |a| + |b|.
\]
By part (e), this gives $|a + b| \leq |a| + |b|$.

content.title: Triangle Inequality Application
content.problem: Prove that $||a| - |b|| \leq |a - b|$ for all $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$.
content.solution:
From the triangle inequality, $|a| = |(a - b) + b| \leq |a - b| + |b|$, 
so $|a| - |b| \leq |a - b|$.

Similarly, $|b| - |a| \leq |b - a| = |a - b|$.

Thus $-(|a - b|) \leq |a| - |b| \leq |a - b|$, which gives 
$||a| - |b|| \leq |a - b|$.

content.title: The Triangle Inequality
content.content:
The triangle inequality is one of the most important tools in analysis. 
Memorize it. Use it constantly. Most estimates in analysis ultimately 
rely on this simple inequality.

content.title: Intervals
content.content:
For $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$:
$$
(a, b) &= \{x \in \mathbb{R} : a < x < b\} && \text{(open interval)} \\
[a, b] &= \{x \in \mathbb{R} : a \leq x \leq b\} && \text{(closed interval)} \\
[a, b) &= \{x \in \mathbb{R} : a \leq x < b\} && \text{(half-open)} \\
(a, b] &= \{x \in \mathbb{R} : a < x \leq b\} && \text{(half-open)}
$$
We also define unbounded intervals:
$$
(a, \infty) &= \{x \in \mathbb{R} : x > a\} \\
[a, \infty) &= \{x \in \mathbb{R} : x \geq a\} \\
(-\infty, b) &= \{x \in \mathbb{R} : x < b\} \\
(-\infty, b] &= \{x \in \mathbb{R} : x \leq b\} \\
(-\infty, \infty) &= \mathbb{R}
$$

content.text:
Note that $\infty$ and $-\infty$ are *not* real numbers; they are 
convenient symbols for describing unbounded sets.
